extra.reasoning:
Dizziness appears in exactly one rule, r5 (priority 100, the highest), which sets acuity to self_care (the lowest level). Therefore enabling dizziness can only ever activate r5, which drives acuity DOWN, the opposite of the required monotonicity.

Concrete counterexample uses the witness assignment shown (the baseline is the same assignment but with dizziness=false):

Baseline (dizziness=false), all other r5 conditions true: severe_headache, palpitations, stiff_neck, focal_weakness, fever, pallor, rash = true; slurred_speech = false. r5 does not fire because dizziness is false. Matching rules include r1 (palpitations->emergent, pri 3), r2 (focal_weakness->emergent, pri 3), r4 (pallor->emergent, pri 3), plus several priority-2 urgent rules. Highest priority matching rule is priority 3 -> acuity = emergent.

After setting dizziness=true (everything else unchanged): all eight conditions of r5 (severe_headache, palpitations, stiff_neck, focal_weakness, fever, pallor, rash, dizziness) are now satisfied. r5 has priority 100, far above every other rule, so it wins -> acuity = self_care.

acuity goes from emergent down to self_care when dizziness is flipped from false to true. This lowers acuity, directly violating the property that setting dizziness true never lowers acuity. (slurred_speech is irrelevant to r5; the violation holds regardless of its value.)
